matches.match_rationale: Campus and class section assignment is a canonical constraint satisfaction problem: students must be placed across sites and year levels while simultaneously satisfying hard constraints (capacity limits, sibling co-location preferences, special needs proximity) and soft constraints (parental preferences, balanced class sizes). This directly addresses the manual placement and last-minute reassignment pain — the optimizer finds valid configurations across hundreds of students that a coordinator could never manually compute. Multi-site operators with 500+ staff typically have the structured enrollment data (applications, sibling links, capacity tables) needed to feed this model.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A constraint-solver engine ingests enrollment applications, site capacities, sibling flags, and year-level quotas to produce an optimized placement roster. Coordinators review and override the proposed assignments via a dashboard, eliminating the manual spreadsheet-driven process and reducing last-minute reshuffling.

matches.match_rationale: Student-to-campus assignment is fundamentally a resource allocation problem — students are entities to be assigned to slots (site + class section) given capacity constraints and demand signals across campuses. This capability directly addresses the coordination challenges between sites on capacity and staffing, since the same model that assigns students can surface projected staffing gaps at each site before the intake period closes. It is technically feasible given standard enrollment management data.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A multi-site resource allocation model continuously balances student headcount across campuses and class sections, triggering staffing reallocation recommendations when projected enrollment at a site diverges from capacity thresholds — replacing manual inter-site coordination emails and spreadsheets.

matches.match_rationale: Capacity miscalculations that cause last-minute reassignments often stem from poor enrollment demand forecasting at the site level. A time series model trained on historical enrollment cycles, application volumes, and sibling cohort data can predict per-campus intake demand weeks ahead of the placement window, giving administrators time to pre-position capacity. This directly addresses the root cause of last-minute reassignments rather than just optimizing after the fact.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An enrollment demand forecasting model produces per-campus, per-year-level intake projections 8–12 weeks before placement decisions are finalized, enabling proactive capacity adjustments (opening new sections, redirecting waitlist applicants) before the problem becomes acute.

matches.match_rationale: A significant portion of the parent frustration pain arises from poor communication around placement decisions — parents receive terse or generic letters with no explanation of why their child was assigned to a particular campus or class section. LLM-generated personalized placement rationale letters, drawing on the assignment data (sibling co-location achieved, preferred site allocated, waitlist position), directly address this frustration at scale without additional staff time.
matches.opportunity_sketch: After the constraint-solver produces placements, a text generation layer automatically drafts personalized parent notification emails explaining the assignment rationale (e.g., sibling placed at same campus, requested site confirmed, waitlist position resolved), reducing inbound parent queries to the admissions team.
